item.title: Two Military Academy Cadets Were Convicted of Killing Adrianne Jones in Texas
item.description: Adrianne Jones, 16, was found shot and killed near Mansfield, Texas on December 4, 1995. The case went unsolved for nearly a year until Naval Academy cadet Diane Zamora confessed to her roommates that she and her boyfriend David Graham, an Air Force Academy cadet, had carried out the killing. Both were arrested in September 1996. Prosecutors said Graham had told Zamora he had been with Jones, and that Zamora demanded Jones be killed. Both were tried separately and convicted of capital murder. Each received a life sentence and is eligible for parole in 2036.

media:content.media:title: Two Military Academy Cadets Were Convicted of Killing Adrianne Jones in Texas
media:content.media:description: Adrianne Jones, 16, was found shot and killed near Mansfield, Texas on December 4, 1995. The case went unsolved for nearly a year until Naval Academy cadet Diane Zamora confessed to her roommates that she and her boyfriend David Graham, an Air Force Academy cadet, had carried out the killing. Both were arrested in September 1996. Prosecutors said Graham had told Zamora he had been with Jones, and that Zamora demanded Jones be killed. Both were tried separately and convicted of capital murder. Each received a life sentence and is eligible for parole in 2036.

item.title: Mark Winger Staged a Double Murder in Illinois and Nearly Got Away With It
item.description: In August 1995, Mark Winger of Springfield, Illinois called police claiming he had shot an airport shuttle driver who had broken into his home and attacked his wife Donnah with a hammer. Authorities initially accepted his account and closed the case. Years later, a friend of Donnah's came forward with information that led investigators to reopen the matter. Evidence including phone records and witness testimony indicated Winger had arranged the meeting himself and staged the scene. In 2002, a jury convicted him of two counts of first-degree murder and he was sentenced to life in prison without parole. In 2007 he received an additional 35 years after being convicted of solicitation of murder for attempting to arrange the killing of a trial witness from prison.

media:content.media:title: Mark Winger Staged a Double Murder in Illinois and Nearly Got Away With It
media:content.media:description: In August 1995, Mark Winger of Springfield, Illinois called police claiming he had shot an airport shuttle driver who had broken into his home and attacked his wife Donnah with a hammer. Authorities initially accepted his account and closed the case. Years later, a friend of Donnah's came forward with information that led investigators to reopen the matter. Evidence including phone records and witness testimony indicated Winger had arranged the meeting himself and staged the scene. In 2002, a jury convicted him of two counts of first-degree murder and he was sentenced to life in prison without parole. In 2007 he received an additional 35 years after being convicted of solicitation of murder for attempting to arrange the killing of a trial witness from prison.
